dominant_error_pattern: Confuses Honig v. Doe (discipline) and Larry P. v. Riles (assessment bias) with Oberti v. Cementon (LRE), treating all foundational cases as interchangeable in scope.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A, B, and D are each central to other doctrinal areas but not LRE. Honig = discipline, Larry P. = testing bias, Rowley = FAPE standard. Only Oberti v. Cementon directly addresses LRE by requiring schools to place students in mainstream settings with appropriate supports before segregating them. Ask candidates: 'Which case asks the presumption-of-inclusion question?'

dominant_error_pattern: Mistakes manifestation determination timing by confusing the Buckley Amendment (records/privacy) with IDEA discipline procedures, or attributing the rule to unrelated cases.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A (Buckley), B (Lau), and C (Larry P.) all relate to student rights or assessment but not discipline manifestation procedures. Honig v. Doe specifically mandates manifestation determination before removal exceeds 10 school days. Teach: 10-day threshold is the trigger; beyond that, manifestation is required. Use a timeline: day 1–10 = possible, day 11+ = manifestation required.

dominant_error_pattern: Treats class-based tracking as a racial segregation issue (Brown) rather than recognizing Hobson v. Hansen's distinct focus on educational tracking by ability or socioeconomic status as a system of educational harm.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor B (Brown) conflates racial desegregation with tracking/ability grouping, which is Hobson's domain. Distractor A (Diane) and D (Larry) are assessment/bias cases, not tracking cases. Hobson v. Hansen established that tracking systems that sort students by race or SES into differential curricula violate equal protection, even without explicit racial language. Teach: Hobson = tracking/access, not racial segregation per se.

dominant_error_pattern: Confuses multiple desegregation and case law precedents by attributing to Hobson or misremembering case names, missing Brown v. Board of Education's singular and foundational role in racial desegregation.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A (Hobson focuses on tracking, not racial segregation), C (Rowley focuses on FAPE/special ed, not race), and D (Lamy P. does not exist or is misremembered) are all incorrect. Brown v. Board of Education is the landmark ruling: separate educational facilities are inherently unequal. This is the foundation for all subsequent integration law. Use: 'Brown = racial segregation is unconstitutional; Hobson = tracking systems are unlawful.'
